title: California Issue – 2026
summary:
2026 The California Issue This year’s California Issue is especially poignant as the Golden State celebrates the 50th anniversary of the Judgment of Paris in the midst of growing existential challenges. We talked to wine industry veterans about the best path forward, hunted for truffles in Paso Robles, paired the best tacos in Los Angeles

The post California Issue – 2026 appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
2026

The California Issue

This year’s California Issue is especially poignant as the Golden State celebrates the 50th anniversary of the Judgment of Paris in the midst of growing existential challenges. We talked to wine industry veterans about the best path forward, hunted for truffles in Paso Robles, paired the best tacos in Los Angeles with California wines, and went down to Valle de Guadalupe to investigate the unsolved mystery of Mexican Nebbiolo.

LA’s Best Wine Bars Are Hidden in Plain Sight

Among the celebrity haunts, old-school classics, and Michelin-star chasers, an unpretentious wine scene is the real reason to love LA.

Read More

Letter from the Publisher

We’re Down With J.O.P (Yeah, You Know Me)

California’s audacity lives on 50 years later, and we need it now more than ever.

In 1976, California was a pip-squeak kid poking the bear of Bordeaux. The sheer audacity it took to sit down at that table and blind taste against the French on their home turf is what we should be drawing inspiration from today. Not the victory. The nerve.

So here we are, at the 50th anniversary of the Judgment of Paris, and I keep asking myself: What are we still trying to prove?

Read More

The real fight isn’t France versus California anymore. Our enemy is bigger and, frankly, scarier. It’s a growing lack of engagement, a culture drifting away from the table, and a generation that hasn’t yet discovered what we already know: Wine is often the connective tissue of social joy.

The 60 Minutes segment “The French Paradox” isn’t coming to the rescue. We never needed France to validate us anyway; we proved that in ’76, and we’ve been proving it every vintage since. California hasn’t just established itself on the world stage; it has set new standards and become an icon entirely on its own terms.

But the next 50 years won’t be won by defending what we’ve built. Today, making great wine is permission to play, not a guarantee you’ll win. California winemakers will succeed by doing what the state has always done best—taking risks, asking questions nobody else is asking, planting grapes nobody thought would work, and ripping out the ones that no longer serve us. The producers I’m most excited about right now are the ones willing to lose it all to find something new.

California’s land is an embarrassment of riches that no other wine region on Earth can replicate. Staggering coastline. Fruit-powering sunshine. A grower culture that is intrinsically down to earth. The Golden State has none of the formality of the Old World, just the authenticity that this moment demands—genuine dialogue, real stories, and a sense of place so vivid it practically speaks for itself.

That spirit lives in these pages. Every story in this issue is a story about people risking it, rethinking it, betting on California the way Chateau Montelena and Stag’s Leap bet on themselves in a Paris hotel in May of 1976. Fifty years later, California’s place in wine history is established. But the unsettled part is the most interesting part. Let’s take these stories as inspiration.

California Dreamin’

title: Winemakers Without Borders: Blurring the Divide Between Alta and Baja California
summary:
The success of Valle de Guadalupe is fueled by vintners who are bridging the Alta-Baja California divide.

The post Winemakers Without Borders: Blurring the Divide Between Alta and Baja California appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
When Tony Viramontes was 18, he left his home in Zacatecas, Mexico, to find work in the United States. He told his mom he’d be back someday.

It took 35 years of climbing Northern California’s corporate ladders, culminating in a VP of Operations role at Jackson Family Wines, but Viramontes is now tending to his homeland. He and his brother, Gerardo Viramontes, also a NorCal wine industry vet, are bringing what they’ve learned in the States to Mexico’s wine regions. And as they’ve done for decades, they continue to help hundreds of workers legally enter the States each year, since labor is in dire demand.

The brothers’ viticultural focus is a 30-acre vineyard in the seaside hills above Valle de Guadalupe in Baja California. “Valle,” as it’s known, is Mexico’s primary wine country, located 70 miles south of the U.S. border and teeming with about 200 estates and five Michelin-starred restaurants.

Rather than settle on the crowded valley floor, the Viramontes brothers are applying their Sonoma Coast know-how to this cooler elevated region, which they’ve been calling Costa de Valle. “I am looking for that Pacific influence,” Tony said on a sunny March morning as we sipped tropical Sauvignon Blanc, savory Petite Sirah rosé, and fresh red blends from their brands West Soul and Juego de Pelota. “I want to farm on the hill and make a different kind of wine.”

Following their typical routine, the brothers, who live in Healdsburg, flew down to San Diego the day before, slept in Tijuana, and drove an hour-plus south that morning to check on their vines. After a lunch of lobster tacos on a bluff overlooking the ocean, they returned to Tijuana that afternoon to deal with workers awaiting visas, and then headed home to Sonoma County. The following week, they’d be in Querétaro, where they manage another vineyard. Crossing the border is their way of life.

“Working between Baja and Alta California has shaped not only how we grow and make wine, but also how we think about continuity, identity, and long-term stewardship in wine regions that are geographically divided yet deeply intertwined,” explained Tony. “Bridging these worlds through vineyards and wine has been central to our work for decades.”

It only took me a few hours in the country to understand that similar bridges span the past, present, and future of Valle de Guadalupe—connections between Mexico and the States, of course, but also Europe, Australia, and South America. This culture of back-and-forth immigration, and the cross-pollination of ideas that it fosters, isn’t just a chapter of the Valle story. It’s the whole book.

“Most of us come from elsewhere,” said Fernando Pérez Castro, a former Mexico City actor who runs the Finca la Carrodilla and La Lomita wineries for his family. “We have people from England, the Netherlands, France, Spain, Italy, all over Mexico. There are a lot of cultures mixed up. You don’t find that in other parts of Mexico.”

This melting-pot environment has given Valle its own rich identity. “We don’t pretend to be Napa or Rioja or Bordeaux,” Pérez Castro said. “We are very proud to be Mexico, and we believe we have everything to offer.”

Homecoming is what brought the Viramonteses back to Valle de Guadalupe, but others came here to find a sense of purpose. Kristin Magnussen was a sandy-haired California girl working restaurant jobs in Santa Barbara when her dad and stepmom bought a small Valle ranch in the early 2000s. They planted the first vines with cuttings from neighbors, harvested grapes with help from volunteer firefighters, and encouraged Kristin to study wine at University of California, Davis.

At first, she declined, but then the scene matured. “The energy was infectious,” said Magnussen, who moved to Valle to run Vinos Lechuza in 2013. “In Santa Barbara, I was just treading water. Let’s give it a go.”

It hasn’t always been easy, especially when her dad died suddenly in 2017, but it’s all working out. “My kids are allowed to be kids,” she said. “We eat well—all of our friends are chefs. We drink well—all of our friends are winemakers. We live such a rich life that when I travel, I want to come home.”

She’s constantly impressed by the freedom that the region’s entrepreneurial spirit allows. “I have the ability to learn and take risks,” she said. “As a female and as an immigrant, that just doesn’t work on paper.”

As a border-straddling gringa, she’s conscious of her responsibility to both sides. “We want to be good ambassadors of Mexico in the United States,” she said, “and we want to be good ambassadors of the United States in Mexico.”

Like Magnussen, Nathan Malagón was born and raised in Alta California, on a ranch near San Juan Capistrano. But his great-grandfather, who was from Guanajuato, had bought land in Valle de Guadalupe in the 1960s. “I was Peter Panning around in my 20s,” said Malagón, who was planning to teach English and surf in Brazil at the time.

Then his dad suggested that Nathan take care of the family ranch in Valle. “This place saved my life,” said Malagón, who rehabbed the 92-year-old vineyard and became entrenched in the community. “It gave me direction.” It also connected him to his wife, winemaker Verónica Santiago, who’d been raised in Ensenada but studied wine in Australia.

They fell in love, launched Mina Penélope in 2014, and now grow Aglianico, Montepulciano, Nebbiolo, Mourvèdre, and Sauvignon Blanc on about eight acres near the coast. While Verónica handles the cellar—a former garage and tool shed under their house—Nathan tends to the vines, ever on the lookout for varieties that can handle this dry region. “The idea here is always: What is more resilient?” he said.

Immigration is what originally ignited Valle de Guadalupe’s agricultural explosion. The wave of Russians who arrived in the early 1900s developed extensive farming operations across 13,000 acres here. Nikolai Rudametkin is a descendant of those settlers. Born in San Diego and raised in Ensenada, he was 16 when his uncle, Fernando Pérez Castro, opened La Lomita and Finca de Carrodilla on their family’s ranches in 2009.

His grandfather paid for Rudametkin to study oenology at UC Davis. “I was an investment,” said Rudametkin, who then worked at Peju and St. Supéry in Napa, Fingers Crossed in Ojai, and Bodega Garzón in Uruguay. He was working in Argentina this spring, and aiming to work a harvest in Champagne this fall.

“That’s how I want to live my life—as an international consultant,” said Rudametkin, who’s proud that his family’s wines were the first in Mexico to be certified organic. “If I become a great winemaker, I’ll just be another great winemaker. But if I become a great viticulturist, I can help everyone become better growers. Then everybody can make better wines.”

That’s been the lifelong mission of Italian-born Camillo Magoni, who arrived in Valle in 1965 and worked for L.A. Cetto winery for nearly half a century before launching his own brand in 2013. “It was like yesterday,” said the beloved Valle pioneer, who introduced stainless steel tanks and Petite Sirah to the region. “It was the best decision of my life.”

His curious spirit never wavered. He now grows a dizzying 121 different varieties across more than 280 acres, which are bottled as Casa Magoni and served beneath a 450-year-old oak tree. His daughter Monica manages operations and his other daughter, Mariela, serves small dishes like sea snail crudo in olive oil and rosemary flowers from the winery’s kitchen.

“We were born in San Diego and raised in Tijuana,” said Mariela, who’s also taught culinary classes in California for 14 years. “But we’re constantly crossing the border.”

Magoni also influenced Tijuana-born winemaker Javier Flores, who works at South Coast Winery in California’s Temecula Valley. Flores’ father worked for Camillo, and so did Javier when his dad got sick with cancer and later died. The younger Flores tried to quit school and work in the cellar multiple times, but Magoni wouldn’t let him and even paid for his education at times.

“I was hiding. I wanted to work,” said Flores, who graduated from Instituto Tecnológico de Tijuana and took a job in winemaking in Temecula in 1989. “[Magoni] said, ‘You’re going! If you don’t leave, I’m gonna kick you out.” He still relies on Magoni for viticultural advice, explaining, “He’s always a phone call away.”

If Magoni epitomizes Valle’s past, Oscar Mancillas is a glimpse into its future. Raised among winemaking families in Ensenada, Mancillas worked in the cellars of Valle legend Hugo d’Acosta at a young age, then studied wine in Spain. For two years, he lived in the Roussillon region of France, producing a brand called Domaine Nada, but then came back to Valle, encouraged by the folks at Bichi in nearby Tecate.

“I had to plant my roots somewhere,” said Mancillas, who launched his brand, Radicantes, in 2023. He’s now running the winery La Escuelita, or “little school,” a nod to the building’s legacy as a classroom of sorts for many Valle winemakers, and continues to consult for estates in the Ramona Valley east of San Diego.

While many Valle wines stick to classic styles, Radicantes bottlings venture into nattier expressions of both freshness and funk. Mancillas is expanding his operations into his family’s vineyard, where he’s planting Grenache Gris, Mission, Colombard, and Sumoll.

This border-crossing culture extends into the region’s cuisine as well. About 45 minutes north of the border in Oceanside, Roberto Alcocer runs the Michelin-starred restaurant Valle. Raised in Ensenada, Alcocer learned English by helping his parents sell trinkets to cruise ship tourists and at weekend swap meets in San Diego, when crossing the border “was so easy.”

He spent years in France learning to cook, eventually landing a high-paying job preparing food for energy-company executives. “I was making good money, but I was seeing my friends become renowned chefs in my country—maybe they weren’t making money, but they were happy and successful,” he said. “Valle was blooming.”

To get in on the action, he opened Malva on the Mina Penélope property in 2014, quickly becoming a must-stop for any Valle visitor. The restaurant closed earlier this year, so he’s been on the search for his next spot, where he’ll be hunting a Michelin star.

“The next Malva is not going to be a palapa,” said Alcocoer. “The next Malva has to show the love and respect I have for Valle. We’ll see another blooming, and I want to be there for it.”

Where to Stay

You can find every type of lodging under the sun in Valle de Guadalupe, from affordable glamping options and small bed-and- breakfasts to fully decked-out resorts.

A luxury leader is El Cielo Resort, developed by a hospitality veteran whose wife is one of Mexico’s top sommeliers. Two-story suites overlook a central lake, and there’s a winery, multiple restaurants, and creative visitor experiences.

Modern, desert-influenced design takes center stage at many hotels in Valle. Bruma Wine Resort is an excellent example, offering single bedrooms all the way up to an entire villa that brings the nature vibes inside.

Centrally located and affordably priced is Surya Hotel & Winery, where the decor recalls the ranchos of old Mexico.

Where to Eat

With 25 Michelin-recommended restaurants in a small area—including five with Michelin stars—Valle de Guadalupe offers a concentrated collection of culinary wonders.

Animalon is considered one of the best restaurants in all of Latin America, while Lunario—owned by the family behind La Lomita and Finca de Carrodilla wineries— does farm-to-table cooking Ensenada style.

For breakfast, everyone recommends La Cocina De Doña Esthela, where the hardest decision is whether to order the corn pancakes or the birria de res, a beefy stew that’s only available on weekends. Get both.

Live-fire cooking dominates the scene, and the pit is certainly the star at Primitivo, a rustic outdoor eatery that shifts its menus based on what meats are available. For a casual wine lunch, try Bodegas Magoni’s curated boards, which showcase local cheeses and meats.

Every resort includes an on-site restaurant, and the most lauded one is Fauna, which is at the Bruma Wine Resort. For a creative twist that blends flavors of Baja with those of the Yucatan, try Latitude 32 at El Cielo.

This article originally appeared in the June/July 2026 California Issue of Wine Enthusiast magazine. Click here to subscribe today!

title: The Best Way to See LA? This Taco and Wine Crawl
summary:
The six best tacos in LA expertly paired with California wines—all worth sitting in traffic for.

The post The Best Way to See LA? This Taco and Wine Crawl appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Pairing the best tacos in Los Angeles with good California wine is arguably the most LA experience you can have. It’s the perfect high-low moment for the modern-day food-and-drink-obsessed person who lives to try the best street food and equally loves the thrill of a spot-on wine pairing at a fine-dining restaurant. So I was determined to create this LA taco-and-wine crawl while keeping it accessible—all the bottles here are under $50.

The tacos I chose for this route are representative of LA’s robust taco universe. Each truck and stand tells the story of an immigrant taquero who has perfected their craft over many years. The wine pairings were curated by sommelier Eduardo Bolaños, a buyer at The Wine House in LA. To make his selections, he took into account the type of tortilla, meat, fish, vegetable, and salsa for each taco, and overcame the challenges that can arise when pairing wine with spicy food.

Like with any meal, the right wine will add depth and nuance to your tacos. People who have eaten these very same tacos many times over may now find themselves savoring entirely different dimensions as their sazón interacts with these wines’ flavors and textures. All the tacos on this crawl are either from street-side operations or take-out-only spots, making it easy to enjoy the pairings at a picnic or back home.

Taco Dorado de Camarón at Mariscos Jalisco

Sauvignon Blanc

Pairing one of California’s most iconic tacos with one of California’s most iconic white grapes seems way too predictable and cliché, but no. In this case, it’s game recognizing game. Press Gang Cellars’ zippy, salty Sauvignon Blanc really leans into the art on its label: a fallen palm tree with a beach in the background. It tastes like you added a teaspoon of lime zest to your glass and serves the same role as a Mineragua would in washing down the bare-minimum order of at least three tacos dorados at Mariscos Jalisco.

The custardy shrimp filling is a secret, but there is an element in there that the wine activates. Could the mystery shrimp binder be a panade base? Could it be potatoes? Could it be instant mashed potatoes? A combination of all three? The founder of this legendary Boyle Heights-style taco truck, Raul Ortega, will take the recipe to his grave, so we will never know. But we can drink more wine until we think we know what the filling is.

Quesataco de Birria de Chivo at Tacos y Birria La Unica

Trousseau

While birria has gone the beef route for both mainstream tastes and cost-effectiveness, Tacos y Birria La Unica has stayed true to birria’s Jalisco roots and still offers a goat version. Theirs isn’t too gamey, with just enough dried red chiles and spice to make the taco one of the city’s most addictive.

To pair, Bolaños wanted something with strong acid and pronounced fruit to stand up to the stew. Adroît’s Trousseau is made by master-sommelier turned-winemaker Chris Miller, who’s based in Monterey. Trousseau isn’t something many vineyards in California are growing right now. It’s the kind of wine that reminds you of every trip to Europe you’ve ever taken. The extremely light body, punchy flavor, and texture are insanely pleasurable and play surprisingly well with this taco.

Al Pastor at Taquería Frontera

Zinfandel

Taquería Frontera’s al pastor taco is easily in the running for the best taco in all of Los Angeles. For the pairing, Bolaños fights fire with fire with a Scar of the Sea Zinfandel from the upstart Cucamonga Valley AVA. Their oaked, dry-farmed, old-vine Zin is fermented with native yeasts and 50% whole cluster, so it’s a natural big boy.

What makes Frontera’s al pastor stand out among the thousands upon thousands of trompos spinning every night in Los Angeles is the taquería’s reluctance to dumb down the chiles in their salsas and adobo. There is no mild option at Taquería Frontera; only full-frontal spice and freshly sliced pineapple. The taquería is descended from the famed Tijuana chain Taco Nazo, and so is their supersecret recipe for creamy cilantro-serrano salsa. The taco’s full-on spiciness and the Southern California Zin battle it out, then call a delicious truce in your mouth.

Battered Fish Taco at Villa’s Tacos #3

Pinot Grigio

While there’s no such thing as California Txakolina, the crisp, lightly effervescent Pinot Grigio from Massican Wines definitely comes close. Massican is the most prominent Napa Valley producer that’s exclusively making white wines. Its 81% Pinot Grigio and 19% Cortese is highly acidic, with a grapefruit flavor that awakens your taco palate.

Victor Villa’s battered mahi-mahi taco can be found at his newest venture, Villa’s Tacos #3. Pinot Grigio almost replaces the need for lime juice in the taco because it does such a good job of balancing the light, flaky batter. The earthy, handmade blue corn tortilla, finely minced purple cabbage, and spicy cashew salsa make this taco a contender for the best fish taco in the city.

Mesquite-Grilled Asada at Tire Shop Taquería

Syrah

Tire Shop Taquería was one of the first spots in LA to offer mesquite-grilled, Tijuana-style carne asada tacos that prioritized high-quality meat, handmade corn tortillas, unwatered-down guacamole, and a roasted tomato salsa that doesn’t hold back.

Syrah itself tends to be smoky, peppery, and meaty, with rich notes of blue and black fruit. This pairing is a feedback loop that enhances both the wine and the taco by bringing out more of their natural flavors. Piedrasassi’s Rim Rock Vineyard Syrah has plenty of tannin and acidity, and spends only a year and a half in a neutral barrel. So there’s not much woody flavor in the wine to overpower the taco.

Potato Crispy Taquitos (Flautas) at Los Dorados LA

Aligoté

Steven Orozco of Los Dorados married into LA’s King Taco family and got the recipe for their insanely addictive red and green salsas. He then zeroed in on the one taco style that no one else in Los Angeles was specializing in: flautas, which means “flutes” in English. His flautas have absolutely zero grease and are smothered in Mexican crema, guacamole, red salsa, and cotija cheese.

A French grape like Aligoté with flautas may seem like an odd match, but it works beautifully here. The crispy-boi nature of Folk Machine’s Aligoté, grown in Mendocino’s Potter Valley, makes it a genius pairing, as it is exactly what you want to drink in between crispy bites of these deep-fried beauties. When lightly chilled, the Aligoté takes on a lovely agua de limón agua fresca profile—fresh and zesty. You can keep drinking it seemingly forever.

This article originally appeared in the June/July 2026 California Issue of Wine Enthusiast magazine. Click here to subscribe today!

title: Proper Wine Storage Matters More in Summer. Here’s How to Do It Right
summary:
Asking an entry-level wine cooler to maintain cellar temperature in a hot room is like asking a window AC unit to cool Madison Square Garden.

The post Proper Wine Storage Matters More in Summer. Here’s How to Do It Right appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Every summer, I end up having the same conversation with wine collectors. Someone notices their wine refrigerator seems to be running constantly, or maybe the temperature is creeping up a few degrees, and suddenly there’s panic about whether their collection is safe. The truth is, summer is one of the toughest times of year for wine storage, and most people underestimate just how much ambient room temperature affects the performance of a wine cooler.

A wine refrigerator can only do so much if the environment around it starts feeling like the inside of a beachside parking garage in August. This is why I always recommend collectors pay attention to the maximum ambient temperature rating of their unit. Not all wine refrigerators are built to handle extreme heat. A cooler sitting comfortably in a climate-controlled dining room may struggle badly in a hot kitchen, garage, sunroom, or pool house once temperatures start climbing into the upper 80s and beyond.

At a certain point, asking an entry-level wine cooler to maintain cellar temperature in a sweltering room is like asking a window AC unit to cool Madison Square Garden. You are setting yourself up for a disappointing experience for your wines, and it’s a great way to melt your Mouton!

In the Shop

Wine Enthusiast Prestige L Dual Zone Smart Wi-Fi Wine Cellar

In Stock | $2,099

Shop Now

This is where premium wine storage units really earn their keep, often handling temperatures in the high 80s to low 90s far better than standard units. Wine hates temperature swings, so that stability matters. Heat accelerates aging, leading to cooked wine that lacks freshness and balance.

And summer heat doesn’t come alone… humidity rises with it. While some humidity is beneficial for wine storage (between 50-80%), excessive moisture can become a problem once levels start creeping above 80%. During these warmer months, you may notice condensation building inside the unit, on shelves, on the floor, or near the door seal. A simple fix is keeping a few dry sponges inside the cooler to absorb excess moisture and regularly wiping down any visible condensation before it builds up. It’s simple maintenance, but it goes a long way toward keeping your cellar environment stable during the dog days of summer.

Seasonal Wines, Seasonal Storage

The funny thing is, summer is also when we tend to enjoy wine the most casually. Suddenly everyone is reaching for Sauvignon Blanc, rosé, Champagne, Albariño, and lighter reds for outdoor dinners and backyard gatherings. Even collectors with cellars full of Napa Cabernet somehow become white Burgundy fanatics between Memorial Day and Labor Day… or at least I do!

If you have a dual-zone unit, the summer months are a great time to think about a seasonal shift in your storage. I always recommend moving your more frequently consumed whites, sparkling wines, and rosés from the long-term storage section into the service zone so they’re properly chilled and ready to go. Nobody wants to wait that painful 15 minutes for a warm bottle of Sancerre to cool down in the freezer while guests hover around the kitchen island pretending not to notice. And if you have ever forgotten about that bottle you tossed in the freezer to cool down, you know what that frozen explosion looks like.

Summer is also when people open their wine refrigerator doors far more often. Between entertaining, barbecues, and spontaneous weeknight dinners outside, your cellar starts getting treated more like the family refrigerator. Every time that door opens in a warm room, not only are you allowing more humidity in the cooler, but your unit has to work harder to recover.

The good news is that protecting your wine during summer usually comes down to awareness more than perfection. Know the conditions of the room where your unit lives and make sure you understand what your particular cooler is designed to handle. Monitor humidity alongside temperature, even with a very basic temperature/humidity gauge. And be sure to give the cooler proper ventilation and keep it out of direct sunlight whenever possible.

Because at the end of the day, wine storage should give you peace of mind, and not become another thing to stress about every time the weather forecast hits 95 degrees. A properly stored bottle of wine rewards patience and preparation, and when the right bottle is opened with the right people around you to share it, those rewards pay off in a big, big way.

title: LA’s Best Wine Bars Are Hidden in Plain Sight
summary:
Among the celebrity haunts, old-school classics, and Michelin-star chasers, an unpretentious wine scene is the real reason to love L.A.

The post LA’s Best Wine Bars Are Hidden in Plain Sight appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Invisible from the street, Baby Bistro is tucked into a nondescript residential lot at the edge of Los Angeles’ Chinatown, where the 101 crosses the 110, in a 1890s Folk Victorian cottage shaded by 15-foot banana plants. With just 35 seats, it’s one of the city’s most in-demand tables, with all the ambition but none of the pretense of a typical destination restaurant. The menu is so short that most tables order every dish. Almost every bottle on the dynamic wine list is under $100, and shorts-wearing walk-ins drink JB Peltier’s “L’Argonnette” Grolleau from the Loire on the front deck.

Co-owner and Chef Miles Thompson has Michelin-starred experience and James Beard Award nominations, yet he’s there daily running the open kitchen, like a friend throwing a dinner party. With tables occasionally sharing bottles, it couldn’t be further from outsiders’ stereotypes of LA as superficial, ostentatious, and driven by money, ego, and celebrity.

While it seems every city’s bar and restaurant scene is chasing awards and best-of lists, LA is looking inward, finding community in a city challenged by the contraction of the entertainment industry, devastating fires, and ongoing population decline, all of which have contributed to bar and restaurant closures. Despite this—or perhaps because LA has a way of being strongest when it’s under strain—it’s exploding with intimate, independent wine bars and restaurants that are more concerned with cultivating neighborhood regulars than global clout. It’s a vibrant and unfussy wine scene that rivals that of any U.S. city.

“LA is a city of classic cocktails, shitty beer, and really great wine,” says Andy Schwartz, Baby Bistro’s co-owner and wine director, while reiterating his love for shitty beer.

The contemporary wine-bar era in LA can be traced back to 2010, with the opening of Bar Covell, where guests chat with the bartender about their tastes and mood rather than looking over a printed wine list. Yet it would be a while before more like-minded wine bars and shops followed in East Hollywood and Silver Lake, which remained the epicenter of modern-minded wine consumption in the city for years.

“When I moved here in 2019, there were some great spots for cocktails but not many smaller, intimate places for wine, just Bar Covell, Augustine, Bar Bandini, and Tabula Rasa,” says Schwartz. “Now you have so many more great spots. Wine bars run by restaurant people, with the hospitality of restaurants.”

Today, Northeast LA—Glassell Park, Eagle Rock, Highland Park, Atwater Village—is one of the most exciting areas in the city for wine. Within a 15-minute drive of each other are Sam’s Place (packed daily despite no website or social media presence), Wife and the Somm, Little Ripper, Capri Club, Justine’s Wine Bar, and Nico’s Bottle Shop with its basement bar, Baby Battista. Along with more indie bottle shops, restaurants with thoughtful lists, and cocktail bars that don’t treat wine as an afterthought, it’s the best place to start your LA wine roaming these days.

On the Westside, don’t miss Travis Lett’s Japanese izakaya RVR, whose beverage director, Maggie Glasheen, was previously wine director at the wine destination Anajak Thai. Force of Nature highlights female winemakers, and Offhand Wine Bar’s list is mostly small California producers. In Chinatown and Downtown, there’s Café Triste from the folks behind the retail shop Psychic Wines, the Roman-inspired Propaganda Wine Bar, Bar Mignon’s generous 4–7 p.m. happy hour, and the all-day café, bottle shop, and wine bar Good Clean Fun.

In the Valley, Augustine Wine Bar, a pioneer that opened in 2016, is back after a two-year closure due to a fire, and the restaurant Anajak Thai still has the best wine list among Thai restaurants outside Thailand. Out in Pasadena, there’s an intimate wine bar in a vegan French bakery, La Cave, and another in a bookstore, The 1894, with over three dozen glass pours.

“Part of why LA’s wine scene is so exciting now is that it’s spread out in all directions,” says Jose Boyer, wine buyer at Capri Club, ostensibly an amaro-focused bar with wine glasses on most tables. “It feels supportive, not competitive, with less fixation on ‘service urgency’ and more of a welcoming hang-out vibe.”

There’s a sense that the best of LA’s wine and food scene is hiding in plain sight. Stir Crazy sits on Melrose Avenue in Hollywood, between flashy Osteria Mozza and the legendary hot- pink hot-dog stand Pink’s. It’s the kind of place you say, “In another city this would be one of its best restaurants,” except that it is one of LA’s best restaurants. It just happens to look like a chic coffeehouse (which it was until Mackenzie Hoffman took it over in 2023).

The dishes at Stir Crazy mark the next wave of California cuisine: sweet potatoes with grapefruit, blue cheese, and fermented raspberry; cabbage with anchovy, Sichuan pepper, and preserved tangerine, all ingredients annotated with their source farms. The list boasts hundreds of wines, yet any staff member can seemingly speak to the intention behind any of them. “You can’t just think of a wine program as being the flag on the yacht,” says Hoffman, who worked at New York’s Four Horsemen restaurant and LA’s influential wine shop Domaine LA before opening Stir Crazy. “It has to be about more than that. It has to be a feeling.”

Hoffman says that the pandemic presented opportunities for wine and food professionals for whom hospitality is a calling, rather than investors and speculators. “Covid pushed out folks challenged by the industry,” says Hoffman. “The people in food and wine now really want to be here.”

Schwartz would agree: “There’s this next generation of restaurant and bar owners, coming in without the money to open something massive, doing most of the work themselves, and landlords willing to work with you,” he says. “There are no longer these industrial mega-restaurants, which can be beautiful in their execution, but I think people want more personal places—hospitality over formality.”

Natural wine is at the heart of most bar and restaurant programs in the city, but it’s rarely labeled as such, and lists are anything but cookie-cutter. “I feel like people coming into wine here aren’t into the ‘shock and awe’ natural wine,” says Schwartz. “They’re principled but not dogmatic, and ultimately want customers to find things they love.” Hoffman agrees: “Before Covid, people were kind of masochistic with their tastes— ‘Give me your funkiest wine’— and now they just want delicious wine made by good people.”

LA has always been a city shaped by cycles of rupture and reinvention, and today it wears its unlikely underdog status well. “New York is the Olympics of the wine world, with all the pressure and competition that comes with it,” says Hoffman. “Here, there’s an approachability that is counterintuitive to what you expect from LA, like, ‘Let’s relax and let people find it.’ We can underpromise and over-deliver.”

This article originally appeared in the June/July 2026 California Issue of Wine Enthusiast magazine. Click here to subscribe today!

The post Berks County Wine Trail appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Berks County Wine Trail

Set among the rolling countryside of Southeastern Pennsylvania, the Berks County Wine Trail brings together a diverse collection of twelve wineries, each offering its own unique approach to winemaking. Established in 2005, the trail has grown into a popular destination for visitors from across Pennsylvania and beyond, showcasing the region’s dynamic and evolving wine scene.

Visit www.berkscountywinetrail.com to learn more

title: At These California Vineyards, Truffles (Almost) Steal the Show
summary:
Estate-grown truffles offer new ways for vineyard owners to diversify their production and income streams.

The post At These California Vineyards, Truffles (Almost) Steal the Show appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
“This is a big one.”

It was a cool January morning at Tufera Farms, in the hills east of Paso Robles, and vintner Austin Hope was carefully clawing through the moist soil with his hands and a long trowel. He’d been digging for nearly 10 minutes since his dog, Nyx, had sniffed out a prized black Périgord truffle.

The farm’s bounty only became apparent recently, seven years after 1,500 holly oak trees—their roots inoculated with the fungus to grow truffles—were planted. The property’s 200-plus acres of vineyard were planted more than 20 years ago, and its Cabernet Sauvignon forms the backbone of Hope’s eponymous bottling.

When the land came on the market last year, he wasn’t looking to buy another vineyard. “But the truffles were here,” says Hope, who was impressed by a 1.8-pound black Périgord that was unearthed there in 2024, likely the largest ever in North America, with an estimated value of $3,800. “I was very jealous.”

After his family purchased the property, Hope trained his duck-hunting dog to search for truffles, and they started hunting together this past winter. On this particular morning, he was joined by many of Paso Robles’ top chefs and restaurateurs, who he hopes will become reliable truffle customers one day.

“We’re in peak season right now,” said Stasi Seay, who manages Tufera Farms. “The cool thing is that you get them so fresh here,” she said. Truffles only have a 48-hour window of optimal flavor, leaving little room for error when shipped from Europe, where they grow in the wild.

After more gentle digging, Hope eventually found the truffle’s edges. It was a whopper. “You didn’t know you were taking a bowling ball out!” Seay said, encouraging everyone to get on their knees and take a whiff of the truffle in situ. “Where else do you get 20 people to stick their heads in a hole?” she laughed. “You’re gonna love it. It’s truffy!”

It was a fortuitous day. Hope and his team, plus Aiko Vail and her German Shepherd, Flora, from Truffle Dog Company, dug up four large truffles. They were cleaned and weighed, and their darkness and marbling were documented; that’s how commercial truffles are scored before they hit the market.

Hope shaved off some slices and served them on bread with truffle butter. Not a bad breakfast for a Monday morning.

Finding the truffles is step one, and it’s a challenge. Tufera Farms is one of three vineyards in California reliably harvesting black Périgords every winter, along with Caelesta Vineyard, also in Paso Robles, and Presqu’ile, in Santa Barbara County.

Only now are these vintners figuring out step two, which is also tricky: How to successfully integrate this highly perishable fungus into your winery business.

Hope plans to properly enter that phase next year, using truffles in products for his recently launched Hope Provisions Co. “We want to take it national,” he said of the new brand, which is already producing estate olive oil, wildflower honey, barbecue rubs, and finishing salts.

Caelesta, just a few miles away from Tufera, is further along in their truffle journey. The Farrell family planted 10 acres of inoculated holly oaks in 2015 and found their first truffles about seven years later. The harvests have grown since then, totaling 35 pounds this year.

“Truffles have such a short life,” said Brian Farrell Jr., who manages the property and also makes the wine. “How do you make them last throughout the year?”

January and February are the heart of the brief season, and this winter he hosted hunts followed by truffle-forward brunches and dinners. “They were sold out by Christmas,” said Farrell, who also leads the occasional truffle cooking demo.

He’s enlisted the chefs at Oak & Vine Catering to make truffle butter. “That’s the one thing I can make and freeze,” he said. “With everybody now, we serve truffle butter and bread.” They’re also tinkering with a truffle cheese idea. “It’s trial and error,” he said.

Farrell is fascinated by how truffles can influence other foods via osmosis, like how eggs stored near truffles become infused with their flavor. He recently visited Cartwright Truffière in Oregon, where he tried regular macarons and then macarons that had sat next to truffles. “They didn’t have that truffle smell or taste at all, but they were 100 times better,” he said. “It’s nature’s MSG.”

Down the coast in the Santa Maria Valley, Presqu’ile Vineyard unearthed 6.5 pounds of truffles this year from their less-than-two-acre orchard. That doesn’t sound like a lot, until you realize that there are 454 grams to a pound, and that a single gram of fresh black Périgord truffle can fetch $5.

Once the Presqu’ile orchard is at full force, they’re expecting about 40 pounds of truffles annually. “That means more than we can possibly use,” said Estate Manager Andrew Heilbrun. Truffles are integrated into the winery’s menu by Chef Julie Simon, and Heilbrun plans to sell the rest to nearby restaurants, like the Michelin-starred Bell’s in Los Alamos.

The rush to plant truffle orchards across California is only intensifying. Few have a closer view of the action than Alana McGee, founder of Truffle Dog Company, which sends teams of experts and dogs around the country to help orchard owners find truffles. She’s seen “a bunch” of new orchards being planted in Northern California and believes the current projects are more likely to harvest truffles than the wave of less meticulous plantings that happened 10 to 15 years ago.

“People who come in now, if they’re getting good advice, they are in a better position to ramp up faster,” said McGee. “That doesn’t mean bucketloads at year five, but they might get some earlier.”

No matter what, the orchards require constant attention. “If you don’t manage it actively, it won’t work,” she said. “There’s not a lot you have to do, but you have to do it, and you have to do it in a certain timeframe. It’s a two-year cycle, so if you don’t, you’ve screwed yourself for two years.”

Jessica and Matt Locklin are learning this quickly. They purchased land with an existing orchard close to Tufera Farms and now manage the project while working full-time in the wine business. The orchard was planted seven years ago, but they haven’t found a truffle yet.

They hope to one day sell their truffles directly to high-end restaurants. “Things are promising, but unless you’re constantly checking the ground and having dogs come out, it’s really hard to know if this is a viable operation,” said Jessica, who works for Paso Robles Wine Country Alliance, noting that every step of the process, from hiring dogs to inoculating the trees, is expensive. “We fund this with our own savings.”

They work with the American Truffle Company, whose cofounder Robert Chang employs cutting-edge research to ensure that his clients’ orchards bear fruit. Of the more than 350 properties across the United States that have tried to grow truffles over the past 40 years, Chang said the vast majority fail. Most of his clients are not vineyards, but he’s currently seeing an “explosion” of vineyards seeking new revenue streams during the wine industry’s slump.

“Truffles are a great way to diversify,” said Chang, who also founded the Napa Truffle Festival in 2010. “Vineyards have the infrastructure. They have the labor. They have the equipment. They have the land. They have the water. Truffles are high-end, and that complements their brands very well.”

He’s worked with prominent Northern California properties like Raymond Vineyards, Peju Winery, and Donum Estate, though none of his wine-country clients have harvested truffles yet. But that hasn’t stopped them from getting wine customers ready. Manzanita Grove Vineyard, for instance, is already hosting truffle events, years before their orchard is likely to be harvested. “People are not waiting until the production of truffles to get into truffle tourism,” said Chang.

Back in downtown Paso Robles, Austin Hope’s morning ended with lunch at BL Brasserie, where Chef Laurent Grangien was nearly giddy at the sight of a just-plucked truffle. He disappeared into the kitchen and returned a few minutes later with a dish of warm beets, hazelnuts, and goat cheese. He shaved the truffle into a generous pile of thin slivers on top. The dish was ambrosial, rich, earthy, and somehow fruity—intriguingly delicious flavors for which words do little justice, much like a fine wine. It only took one bite to realize what all this fuss is about.

This article originally appeared in the June/July 2026 California Issue of Wine Enthusiast magazine. Click here to subscribe today!

title: In the War Against Lanternflies, East Coast Wineries Are Getting Creative
summary:
As spotted lanternflies continue to torment vineyards, vintners and community members are coming up with creative solutions and retaliating together.

The post In the War Against Lanternflies, East Coast Wineries Are Getting Creative appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Nothing brings a community together quite like a common enemy—and the wine community has a lot of enemies at present: tariffs, inflation, climate change, anti-alcohol sentiment, changing consumer behavior, and plain-old competition from an increasing number of beverage options. With wineries as with Hollywood, though, the more compelling battle often isn’t the economic, cultural, or even meteorological one, but the one fought against a hostile alien invasion.

Enter: spotted lanternflies. An antagonist with as dramatic a costume as the best cinematic villains, this invasive insect species is believed to have arrived on our shores in 2012, were first identified in Pennsylvania in 2014, and have wreaked havoc in certain Northeastern wine regions and vineyards since the early 2020s.

“Their primary food source is the tree of heaven—also an invasive species,” says Beth Erickson, President and CEO of Virginia’s Loudoun Convention & Visitors Association, where spotted lanternflies have enthusiastically taken up residence. “Their secondary food source is grapevines.”

The First Attack

Through nutrient-sucking, sap-secreting, and other destructive behaviors, spotted lanternfly populations are affecting everything in wineries from vine health and fruit quality to visitor experience. 

The first wave of doing battle with these winged tormentors involved a lot of crossed fingers, in places like the Finger Lakes to the Garden State and beyond. “It blew up pretty quickly,” says Dustin Tarpine, chairman of Garden State Wine Growers Association, and co-founder of Vinetech Vineyard Management Company, “and it took a few years to figure a lot of stuff out.”

Armed with some input from various academic programs and state departments of agriculture, many wineries with initial infestations resorted to various sprays to try to keep the bugs down. “The same materials that we would use to control the few pests that we do have, fortunately do work on lanternflies as well,” says Tarpine, though those methods can prove a quandary for organic wineries, or those whose ethos would typically prohibit them. “We generally don’t use insecticides but may be forced to this year,” says Michael Migliore, co-owner of Hudson Valley’s Whitecliff Vineyard and Winery.

Meanwhile, the introduction of would-be lanternfly predators to vineyards has had mixed results. “If I could figure out a way to make lanternflies more attractive to chickens, I would,” says Scott Spelbring, head winemaker at Bluemont Vineyard, an extension of Virginia’s Great Country Farms, which already has the birds in place. “[The chickens] haven’t really wanted anything to do with them.”

Scraping the lanternfly egg sacks before they have a chance to release nymphs is also a common strategy, though a labor-intensive one for vineyard workers. “Our vineyard stewards are the first line of defense; ensuring they are properly trained to identify and manage spotted lanternflies across all life stages is essential,” says Leo Mora, production winemaker at Long Island’s Sparkling Pointe Vineyards & Winery. “To support this, the team at Cornell Integrated Pest Management and Raul E. Lemus Garza, (Bilingual Project Specialist) have developed Spanish-language pest management resources and training,” he says, which have been attended by the vineyard teams of various wineries in Long Island and beyond.

Increasing Consumer Awareness

As lanternflies prove both a nuisance to the public at-large and a frequent source of headlines, consumers are also becoming more aware of them, which helps the cause, to a degree. “Finger Lakes gets a lot of tourists from areas that are already infested with spotted lanternflies,” says Shannon Brock, co-owner and estate manager of Silver Thread Vineyard, “so whenever the topic comes up, I encourage people to get their cars washed before driving up here so they don’t inadvertently bring eggs masses with them.”

In other good news, many of the wineries in the northeast that first did battle against these bespeckled predators have seen a drop in numbers after the initial infestation. “The issue has abated for us a bit,” says Tarpine. “So far it’s followed a pretty consistent pattern: they come in, you have a three-ish year period where it’s bad, and then they tend to migrate out somewhat.”

“I would say 2023 was probably the peak of how bad it got for us,” Tarpine continues. “We’re still seeing them every year, but it’s something that we’re just kind of living with now; it’s not like the issue that it was, or quite the volume that it was a couple years ago.”

As certain wineries are now in the declining stage of lanternfly infestations, others are gearing up for their own battles, better armed and benefitting from all that has already been learned.

“Many vineyards, including ours, have bug traps around their plantings to monitor for spotted lanternflies,” says Brock. “Most vineyards have been aggressively cutting down tree of heaven on their properties in order to reduce potential populations, and all of our vineyard employees have been trained in spotting egg masses and the different stages of the insect…. As a practicing organic vineyard, we purchased an organic insecticide that was effective last season so that we could gain experience using it in advance of the expected infestation.”

Loudoun County’s ‘Scrape for the Grape’ Initiative

“I was walking my dog, and there was a couple having photos taken right after their wedding,” recalls Erickson. In Loudoun County, Virginia—D.C.’s wine country—wineries are a common setting for such celebrations. “But then the bride drops her groom’s hand, steps away, lifts up the hem of her gown, and stomps on a spotted lanternfly,” she recounts. “And as she did it, she said, ‘This is for the vineyards.’”

The realization that visitors were already built-in allies in the war on lanternflies, and a source of potential community action, was an “aha” moment for Erickson and Loudoun. “We were coming into the 40th anniversary of wine in Loudoun County in 2024, and we knew that we were going to be marking that, and were trying to figure out a way to connect and to really make people aware that we truly did need all hands on deck,” she says, after spotted lanternflies came on pretty rapidly to the area in 2023, progressing southward and westward from the northeast’s initial infestations.

Partnering with Loudoun Invasive Removal Alliance, (LIRA) an organization founded in 2023 which represents 86 homeowners associations, 16 wineries and vineyards, and Ida Lee Park, Loudoun’s Scrape for the Grape initiative was born, gathering volunteers to sift through vineyards and nearby parks before budbreak to destroy egg masses before they hatch.  

“It’s the most disgusting Easter egg hunt,” Erickson says.

Building a Lanternfly-Destroying Army

Like any good egg hunt, however, Scrape for the Grape does bring people out en masse, growing from just over 400 volunteers in its first year in 2024, to more than 1100 volunteers in 2026. Between LIRA, Loudoun Wildlife, and Virginia Tech’s Virginia Cooperative Extension, webinars were developed to educate volunteers both about the spotted lanternfly lifecycle, and the identification of the tree of heaven. Volunteers can sign up to work at a specific winery, or be assigned to one, where they’ll work in small teams to identify, count, and destroy egg masses, using a plastic card to scrape them off of the vines and crush them in the process.

At Bluemont Vineyard, Spelbring hosts 50 volunteers on each of the three Scrape days. “People are usually squeamish about bugs, but to see families, kids, dogs, and everybody come out for this, it’s really been impactful,” he says. “For some people, it’s the first time they’ve actually had their hands on vines, so we get a lot of questions and it’s actually been a lot of fun.” With that hard work comes rewards for participants: Volunteers may be treated to cider and donuts, discounts, or free wine tastings on the Scrape for the Grape days, or ongoing discounts and promotions throughout the season.

The reality of lanternfly lifestyle, however, is that the number of destroyed egg masses in a vineyard doesn’t necessarily correlate to a decrease in lanternfly adults present in that same vineyard later in the season. “My understanding is they do like to switch food sources, so they may start in the vineyard, and then they’re like, ‘all right, we’re bored here,’ and then vice versa,” Spelbring says. 

Regardless, based on volunteer-reported counts of egg sacks destroyed, using a conservative average of about 30 eggs per sack, “over the three years of the program, there have been over 2300 volunteers who have come out in total, and they have eliminated nearly 20 million spotted lanternflies,” says Erickson. “No matter where they are, they certainly don’t recognize any kind of boundaries,” she says, “so the more that can be removed out of the entire ecosystem, the better for all of us.”

Whether or not scraping has made a difference in the number of lanternflies found in vineyards, “the big benefit that’s come out of all of this is the sense of community and overall awareness,” says Spelbring. “It strengthens the ties between the winery and the visitors.”

Erickson agrees, pointing out that “the wineries have all seen an increase in their wine club memberships,” since they have a personal, tangible stake in the success of the winery for whom they’ve done battle. 

Meanwhile, Erickson and the various partner organizations are preparing Scrape for the Grape to be a blueprint for other regions and wineries in the future, knowing that Loudoun’s infestation will eventually taper off, and invasions elsewhere will amp up. 

“I’ve spoken with my colleagues in other regions, and everybody’s watching for it,” she says, even as far away as California. “It’s just a drop in the wine barrel, if you will,” she says, “but it’s awareness, it’s engagement, it’s all the things about volunteering and sustainability that matter.”

  • New research suggests that organic and biodynamic farming can actually improve yields and resilience.

title: Is Mexican Nebbiolo… Actually Nebbiolo?
summary:
The vast differences between Nebbiolo in Mexico and Piedmont have led winemakers and industry pros to wonder if they’re even the same grape.

The post Is Mexican Nebbiolo… Actually Nebbiolo? appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
One gorgeous spring day in Valle de Guadalupe, as a band played a romantic Mexican folk song, I tasted two Nebbiolos under a 450-year-old oak tree at Casa Magoni winery. Or perhaps I should say I tasted one Nebbiolo and one “Nebbiolo”—in big air quotes.

I was with the 85-year-old winemaker Camillo Magoni, who poured two different bottlings. First, his 2016 Nebbiolo Clone 34, grown from vines native to Valtellina in Italy’s Lombardy region, where Magoni was born. Next came his 2019 Nebbiolo de Baja. The difference was stark: Clone 34 had the light color, finesse, and rose and cherry notes of a young Piemontese Nebbiolo. The Nebbiolo de Baja was darker, more muscular, and more brooding. I enjoyed both, but the question was unavoidable: How were these two wines from the same grape variety?

“Of course they are different,” Magoni said. “But I never have a preference as long as the wine is well made.”

Other Baja winemakers are more blunt about their skepticism toward the so-called Nebbiolo that grows in Baja. “Some people say that it’s Dolcetto or Lambrusco,” said Lulú Martinez Ojeda, winemaker at Bruma, one of Valle de Guadalupe’s most highly regarded producers. “All I can say is it’s not Nebbiolo. I love it, but it’s not Nebbiolo.” Martinez Ojeda says a Mexican-American sommelier in New York once asked about her wine, “So is this Nebbiolo, or is this Nebbio-cholo?”

So, if the Nebbiolo in Valle de Guadalupe is not really Nebbiolo, then what is it?

Like most up-and-coming wine regions, Valle de Guadalupe has struggled to find a signature grape. The region’s drought conditions and heat lead to a lot of experimentation, and many vineyards grow Chardonnay and Cabernet Sauvignon. Some grow very good Chenin Blanc. Some believe Rhône varieties like Mourvèdre and Grenache do best. Others veer toward Spanish varieties like Tempranillo. Others still insist on Italian grapes like Sangiovese, Montepulciano, and Aglianico.

But for now, the signature grape in Valle de Guadalupe is Nebbiolo. L.A. Cetto, the region’s big legacy producer, has promoted Nebbiolo as the area’s main variety since the 1980s, and it’s been in Baja for at least seven decades. After World War II, many varieties were imported here from Piedmont, including Barbera, Grignolino, Freisa, and “one variety they named Nebbiolo,” Magoni said, with a smirk.

Magoni came to Baja in the 1960s after working in vineyards in Valtellina, a valley in Italy’s Lombardy region, where Nebbiolo is the local grape. “Nebbiolo is probably the variety I know better than any other, so when I arrived in Baja and visited the wineries, I was a little bit shocked,” he said. “Of course, I had my ideas. But I shut my mouth and just observed and didn’t talk.”

The Nebbiolo you taste in Baja is quite different from what you’d recognize in Italian wines made from it, like Barolo or Barbaresco. It’s darker, less tannic, and often has a briny, saline character. Maybe it’s the climate, or the result of growing Nebbiolo in a drought, where the water that remains on the valley’s ocean-bed soil becomes saltier and poorer in quality.

“It’s not Italian. There’s more color, more intensity,” said Paolo Paoloni, an Italian winemaker working for Villa Montefiori in Baja. Paoloni calls his Nebbiolo de Guadalupe. Meanwhile, Magoni has adopted the name Nebbiolo de Baja, presumably in case some DNA testing reveals the grape’s true identity.

Kristin Magnussen at Vinos Lechuza recently released a Nebbiolo aged in stainless steel and sold in a can. She offered a hypothesis: “In Piedmont, it’s a light- skinned grape used to fog. But here, they’re in direct sunlight. Maybe they’ve evolved to have a thicker skin,” she said. “Is it really Nebbiolo? Well, it’s Mexican Nebbiolo.”

“Nobody expects anything mainstream from Mexico, so we can make whatever we want,” said Martinez Ojeda. We were sipping her vibrant Casa Jipi Nebbiolo, which is bursting with fresh red fruit and a touch of minerality. The Nebbiolo for Casa Jipi comes from a vineyard planted in 1978. “Casa Jipi is definitely a Nebbio-cholo. At this point, it’s just a part of us.”

There is a long tradition of grapes being called by confusing local names, especially with Italian varieties. Sangiovese is variously called Brunello, Morellino, Prugnolo Gentile, and two dozen other names. Nebbiolo is called Chiavennasca in Valtellina and Spanna in Gattinara.

Cases of grape identity crisis happen more often than you’d think. The most famous was in Chile, where they discovered that the strange Merlot they were growing was actually Carménère. A similar discovery happened in Colli Berici, in Italy’s Veneto. There, red wines once thought to be Cabernet Franc also turned out to be Carménère, based on DNA testing. While some wineries embraced Carménère as a new signature grape, not all local winemakers were happy about this revelation. Many were Carménère deniers who continued to bottle their wines as Cabernet del Veneto.

Something similar may soon happen in Valle de Guadalupe. I spoke with José Vouillamoz, one of the world’s foremost grape geneticists and co-author of the groundbreaking encyclopedia Wine Grapes. He said at least one DNA test on Baja Nebbiolo showed it could be the grape Lambrusco di Alessandria, which has origins in Piedmont’s Alessandria province. However, Vouillamoz made clear that this was only one sample from one vineyard, and that more testing would be needed before anything definitive could be established. “I can’t say anything until I see the results of DNA profiling,” he said. “Maybe let them know I do perform DNA testing on a regular basis.”

“So why hasn’t there been a DNA test?” said Martinez Ojeda. “Well, that’s very expensive. But maybe it’s time.” She added, “If you find out that it’s not Nebbiolo—that it’s Dolcetto or Freisa or whatever—then what? Then what do you do?”

Back at Casa Magoni, as our tasting under the old oak tree moved into a leisurely afternoon of wine drinking, I proposed a similar question: “So, have you done DNA testing?

Magoni laughed. “Yeah,” he said.

“And?”

“Who cares?” he said with a shrug.
